text.title: QA Suite Health Metrics Canon — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `qa-suite-health-metrics-canon` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a canon from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: The qa suite health metrics canon has a fixed section list with named headings. Practitioners fill sections; they do not invent new ones per use.

rationale.source: Standardisation literature (Atul Gawande, The Checklist Manifesto) and information-architecture canon

rule.rationale: Variable structure across instances destroys comparability and prevents future audit or aggregation. A fixed shape makes filled artefacts diff-able and reviewable.

rule.statement: Every claim or recommendation in the template carries a citation field (link, ticket, transcript ID, or measurement). Unevidenced sections are flagged at review.

rationale.source: Evidence-based delivery practice; Lean Startup validated-learning rules

rule.rationale: Templates without evidence anchors decay into opinion-capture. Tagging evidence at fill-time makes the artefact defensible and reusable in retro / audit.

rule.statement: The header carries `version`, `last_reviewed`, and a named owner. Templates without ownership age into untrusted folklore.
rule.rationale: Templates ship as living docs. Anonymous templates rot because no one is on the hook for refresh. A named owner pins refresh accountability.

rule.statement: The fill budget is explicit: target ≤ 30 minutes for the QA engineer. Templates that exceed it are split into a short variant + an extended variant.

rationale.source: Cognitive-load research; field experience with operational templates

rule.rationale: If filling the template costs more than the artefact yields, practitioners route around it. A budgeted template enforces leverage.

rule.statement: Each filled instance ends with a `decisions / actions / next-review` block. Templates without an action block are reading material, not operating tools.

rationale.source: Decision-journal practice (Annie Duke, How To Decide); GTD next-action discipline

rule.rationale: code-coverage methodology exists but is single-metric. The user's pain 'coverage games reality' demands a canonical four-metric view (pass rate, duration, flake rate, changed-lines coverage) and explicit anti-patterns to avoid.
